TextENGNew: Satoko went off to join the children playing in the schoolyard.
I was not in that sort of mood, so I saw Satoko off and decided to stay behind.
changeReason: New translation adds 'saw Satoko off' (沙都子を見送り) which was omitted from the original, providing the detail that the narrator specifically saw Satoko leave.

TextENGNew: ...She probably wouldn't be home until evening.
When Satoko isn't around... I have ways of spending the time befitting her absence.
changeReason: New translation shifts from past tense to present tense matching the Japanese, and 'befitting her absence' (いないなりの) conveys a more deliberate, specific way of spending time than the original's generic 'without her.'

TextENGNew: Besides, I was in that sort of mood.
changeReason: Original adds an interpretation ('what I wanted to do') not in the Japanese; the new translation accurately conveys そういう気分だった ('was in that sort of mood'), which is vaguer and more atmospheric.

TextENGNew: I thrust my hand deep into the pile of winter futons at the back of the closet and pulled it out.
changeReason: Original says 'pulled one out' (implying a futon), but the Japanese それを ('it') refers to a hidden object behind the futons; the new translation's 'pulled it out' correctly identifies a specific concealed item.

TextENGNew: Then I packed a rather elegant glass full of ice made in the freezer.
changeReason: New translation adds 'made in the freezer' (製氷室で作った) which was omitted from the original, and this detail is important as the freezer ice is a key plot point discussed in the next lines.

TextENGNew: But ice from the freezer smells of chlorine and tastes unpleasant.
Cracked ice would be best, but Satoko pesters me asking what I need it for, so lately I haven't been able to buy any.
changeReason: かち割り氷 means 'cracked/crushed ice,' not 'store-bought ice'; the new translation corrects this specific term.

TextENGNew: ...If coming up with a convincing excuse would let me be rid of this chlorine stench for all eternity, well, that does seem worth some deliberation.
I filled it with orange juice... then poured in a measured amount of the other liquid.
changeReason: Old says 'mineral water' but Japanese says オレンジジュース (orange juice) -- wrong drink.

TextENGNew: Watching the orange liquid mingle with the red and gradually deepen to a reddish-black — it was a quietly elegant sight.
changeReason: Old reduces vivid color description (orange, vermillion, reddish-black) to 'transparent water.'

TextENGNew: Apparently the two ingredients cancel each other's best qualities, but this is exactly the taste I like, and I don't want to hear complaints about it.
changeReason: Original vaguely says 'this wasn't the right way to drink it,' while the Japanese specifically states お互いの素材のよさを潰しあっている (the ingredients cancel each other's good qualities), which the new translation accurately conveys.

TextENGNew: The two fruits together have just the right sweetness and fragrance, bringing on a pleasant tipsy sensation (?) much like genuine fruit wine.
changeReason: Old bears no resemblance to the Japanese about two fruits creating a tipsy feeling like fruit wine.

TextENGNew: With no alcohol in it at all, calling it a buzz may be a bit presumptuous.
changeReason: Old is entirely unrelated to the Japanese about it being presumptuous to call non-alcoholic drinking 'intoxication.'

TextENGNew: Thanks to that, it lets me enjoy a small sense of transgression — which isn't bad at all, when you think of it that way.
changeReason: Old invents 'enjoy a single bottle for a long time'; Japanese says 背徳感を楽しめる (enjoy a sense of transgression).

TextENGNew: I spread a cushion at my favorite spot by the window... and with the glass in hand, savored the familiar view and the wind stroking my hair as accompaniment.
A faint, sweet, well-ripened fragrance tickled my nose.
...Just as I was about to settle into this tranquil moment and enjoy it, I sensed the noisy one returning.
It seems this habit of mine is something she simply cannot abide, and every time I tilt the glass to my lips she starts complaining.
Sure enough, she began making a loud, scolding fuss.
"...Oh, be quiet. Learn some tolerance. What I drink or eat is my own business."
"B-But you can't...! That drink is still too early for you...!"
changeReason: Old explicitly mentions 'alcohol' not in the Japanese, which deliberately keeps the drink's nature ambiguous.

TextENGNew: I tried to simply ignore her, but she started making a racket as if bent on ruining the atmosphere entirely.
...There's no way I can enjoy the feeling at all like this. When will she ever notice this isn't actually alcohol?
changeReason: Old omits critical second sentence about when she'll realize the drink isn't actually alcohol.

TextENGNew: Stifling an inward laugh while wearing an irritated expression, I hurled the contents of the glass out the window.
changeReason: Original omits 内心苦笑しながら (while inwardly laughing), which reveals the narrator's true amused reaction beneath the irritated facade—an important characterization detail.

TextENGNew: "...Want to know why I'm feeling gloomy? ...It's because the manner of my death has been decided."
It seemed that at last even she understood why I had wanted to lose myself in this particular mood.
changeReason: Old says 'drown myself in alcohol' but Japanese describes immersing in a mood; the drink is non-alcoholic.

TextENGNew: "You don't need to look so despondent. Death will be fairly instantaneous, so it won't be all that frightening."
My corpse will be charred and dismembered, but the moment of dying itself will be instantaneous, so it falls somewhere in the acceptable range.
changeReason: Original omits まぁまぁの部類だ ('falls in the acceptable range'), which reveals Rika's desensitized attitude toward her own death—an important characterization detail for the time-loop narrative.

TextENGNew: ``...But... again... it won't work out.``
changeReason: Original translates as 'not yet,' but the Japanese また、駄目 means 'again, it's no good/it won't work out,' implying repeated failures (time loops). 'Not yet' suggests future possibility, while 'again it won't work out' conveys resigned repetition.

TextENGNew: ``I know. ...Again it won't work. ...So when is my day of death this time?``
changeReason: Original translates また駄目 as 'not yet' (same issue as previous line), and 命日はいつだっけ as 'when am I going to die again' rather than 'when is my day of death this time'—the new translation better conveys Rika's clinical, resigned inquiry about a predetermined death date.

TextENGNew: "...I believe it is the night of the twenty-fifth of June."
"About a week after Watanagashi ends. ...Well, that's about what I'd expect."
"Is it... after all... my fault?"
"Obviously. Have you no self-awareness?"
changeReason: Original translates 自覚ないの as 'Are you serious?' but the Japanese specifically asks about self-awareness (自覚); the new translation preserves this pointed accusation about lacking self-awareness of one's own culpability.

TextENGNew: She was the one who brought it up, yet when I confirm it she goes teary-eyed. ...Honestly, what a smothering creature.
changeReason: Original translates 暑っ苦しいやつ as 'annoying,' but the Japanese literally means 'suffocating/smothering person'; the new translation's 'smothering creature' better captures Rika's exasperated but not entirely hostile characterization.

TextENGNew: "Let's do our best. ...Surely in the next round of the game, sixes will come up one after another."
June 25th.
...There are still more than ten days. I'll spend them at my own leisurely pace.
Will she ever notice, before then, that this imitation wine isn't actually wine at all...?
changeReason: Old misses the punchline: Japanese asks whether she'll realize the fake-alcohol isn't alcohol.
